g.data-narration: Welcome to this lecture on Remote Method Invocation (RMI) - a fundamental Java technology for distributed computing.
RMI is a Java API that allows objects running in different JVMs to communicate as if they were in the same virtual machine.
g.text: A Java API enabling method calls on objects running in
different JVMs - possibly on different machines across a network.

g.data-narration: RMI enables location transparency - clients access remote objects using names, making distributed programming as simple as local calls.
g.text: Why RMI Matters in Distributed Computing
RMI enables building client-server applications where objects can
communicate seamlessly across network boundaries using Java.

g.data-narration: RMI follows a four-layered architecture: Application, Stub/Skeleton, Remote Reference, and Transport layers working together.
The five key RMI components are Remote Interface, Implementation, Stub, Skeleton, and the RMI Registry for name lookups.
RMI works by having clients lookup remote objects, then stubs serialize and send method calls across the network to skeletons.
Every remote interface must extend java.rmi.Remote and declare all methods to throw RemoteException for network failure handling.
g.text: int add(int a, int b) throws RemoteException;

g.data-narration: The RMI Registry binds objects to names on port 1099, allowing lookup, rebind, and unbind operations for object management.
To create an RMI app: define the remote interface, implement the remote object, create server, create client, and run with registry.
A complete RMI example includes Calculator interface, CalculatorImpl implementation, CalculatorServer, and CalculatorClient.
RMI uses distributed garbage collection with a lease-based system - remote objects have leases that clients must renew to stay alive.
RMI provides location transparency, type safety, automatic serialization, object passing, and built-in garbage collection for distributed objects.
RMI limitations include Java-only support, performance overhead from serialization, and less suitability compared to modern distributed frameworks.
In summary, RMI is a powerful Java technology for distributed computing with four-layer architecture enabling transparent remote method calls.
g.text: RMI enables transparent distributed computing in Java through stubs, skeletons, and the RMI Registry
